Discussed clients needs around market research. He really wants to understand the market for his services. He does compliance work for companies exporting to the European Union.


He commited to giving me additional information so we can discuss later.


(Note that this session was delayed in entry due to an issue with his account in Salesforce)

Analysis

Samantha currently has one major client who brings in approximately $1,000 per week, making this a significant source of income for her. However, despite the financial benefit, the relationship has become increasingly toxic. During our conversation, she spent nearly an hour venting about the challenges and emotional toll this client has caused. More concerningly, she expressed feeling fearful of the client’s right-hand man, which has created an environment of stress, anxiety, and even concern for her safety.

While this client is currently her biggest income stream, the emotional and psychological cost is becoming too high. No client is worth compromising personal safety or peace of mind. She has also recently begun to gather more clients, giving her a bit more financial flexibility.

Recommendation

Given the serious nature of her concerns—particularly regarding safety—I recommended that she carefully plan her exit fro

This session was a status update. The client has been working on opening a bar in downtown Kalamazoo. Although all parties, landlord, shared tenant and the client are friendly they are not sure how to allow the new business to use the liquor license. After reviewing the plan and hearing about all the family members involved in the business that owns the liquor license, I suggested contacting John Schmitt to consult on the transfer and advise as to an attorney.

Hi Shawn,


I am so sorry I did not get back to you months ago. I think I was out of town getting ready for surgery and meant to respond. 

At any rate, if you haven't heard, my father passed away recently. It was quick and he worked right up until he passed. 

We were a week away from signing some trust and will papers and unfortunately have been in the probate process until just this week. My youngest brother Tom and I have been managing the estate and business. 


My dad had begun to move forward with the idea of selling the business after not getting any further with my brother on a decision of whether he was committed or able to take the business over. We had met with a potential buyer who was connected to us through a friend, but we have not officially advertised it for sale. That said, we are now in a position of needing to sell or re-

Appreciate you reaching out Kate, very kind. 


Things are going ok. Currently just figuring out what I want to do going forward. I have a potential project with a local builder, but nothing in stone yet. 


Given the current economic climate, disposable income will be tight for many in regards to custom furniture. I saw that as well even before this year…so I’m not sure exactly my next steps or if there will be next steps. 


After some thought, I don’t think that what I’m making or have the skillsets to make is anything that people are seeking. So perhaps I’ll need to pivot into something else…and I’m not sure what that is yet exactly.


I do appreciate you being kind enough to check in though! I hope you’re doing great on your end! Saw you’ve been putting some time into the social media things and that looks like it’s doing well for ya!

During our meeting, the client shared plans to launch a solo rideshare business using a luxury vehicle. We covered the following key topics:


  • Startup Costs: Reviewed anticipated initial expenses, including vehicle purchase/leasing, licensing, insurance, and branding.
  • Monthly Operating Costs: Discussed recurring expenses such as fuel, maintenance, insurance, and platform fees.
  • Revenue Forecast: Outlined potential income based on service rates, target clientele, and projected ride volume.
  • Market Research: Emphasized the importance of researching the local rideshare landscape to identify service gaps and competitive opportunities.
  • Business Registration: Reviewed steps for registering with the state as a Transportation Network Company (TNC).

Met with David at the Can Do Kalamazoo office as a follow up to his business plan for the computer repair coffee shop concept. He has changed his plan and is interested in purchasing a franchise. Healthy You vending machines is a low start up cost vending business that works well as a second income with his security guard job. He has applied for funding from the company and was turned down (no down payment, no assets for collateral and low credit score). The initial investment for 3 machines is about $40,000. We discussed preparing him to go to his bank and ask about a possible loan or HELOC etc. He still needs to write a business plan for the vending business. He will be gathering more info and looking into where he can put the machines in Kalamazoo. There is currently no HealthyYou machines in Kalamazoo County.

Jasper,

My thanks for your time yesterday and walking me through more of your goal for the number of documentaries you will produce from this grant. I hope you found it helpful to talk through the "math" of it all. 


At the moment, you're considering getting to 2, 25-minute documentaries with the grant funds acting as a "reimbursement" for your expenses in producing the videos. Essentially, the grant won't cover your time but will cover all the expenses. I realize this is not your optimal solution. I do believe that it could be a building block for your speaking gigs in the future. 


I appreciate your deep knowledge of the scientific basis for why we have bias and how to ta

Jeff is navigating several interconnected challenges as he builds and pitches his product, Stampede Social. A key hurdle is effectively communicating the value of the platform in a market where many potential users undervalue social media’s role in conversion and sales. Jeff also faces resistance from clients who are hesitant to change old processes or commit to trials, as well as a broader issue of limited visibility and attribution in influencer marketing. These obstacles are compounded by website design inconsistencies and the difficulty of positioning a data-rich platform in a way that feels both intuitive and credible to time-starved users. He’s also balancing outreach efforts, fundraising, and pitch development while considering how to structure a usage-based trial model that aligns with user behavior.


I recommended Jeff restructure the website flow to better tell a conversion-oriented story—starting with an intro, followed by a “villain” section (focused on h

I met with our client by zoom call. The main point that she wanted to discuss was whether it would be worth it to set up a company, which of course is that it all depends. I encouraged her to consider what amounts of revenue she could generate from 2 basic revenue streams: custom photography and life coaching. We discussed that we would not expect operating expenses would be too high and that it really is a function of what revenues she could bring in. To that end I shared a copy of "Basic Bookkeeping 2.0" and encouraged her to speculate as to how much revenue she could generate given the hours she has available after working her full time job. I then asked her to think about all the various expenses she might encounter running such a business. She would get back to me when she has completed her projections for the year so that we could review them and consider whether it is feasible for her to move forward setting up a company. I walked her through the process of setting

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Zoom call with Mohan. Discussed his roll-out plan for MVP which includes rolling the product out at multiple campuses and his need to idetnify both key resources (drivers, tutors, etc.) as well as students needing these services. He still struggles with finding a key value proposition for the process and effective mechanism for outreach. Also discussed concerns regarding potential liability/safety concerns with offering rides, etc. and discussed how he would vet the servide providers. Encouraged hin to focus on one locale instead of many, where he can have a high level of direct involvement.


We also discussed progress with his financial projections and financial viability.

Melissa has been feeling overwhelmed because she has so many ideas and can't seem to narrow them down to one business idea. She kept saying that she wants to do something economical, and talking with her about the commissary kitchen route seemed to resonate with her vs rebuilding and licensing her space with MDARD. We started on her Liveplan again, but were having a lot of technical difficulties so I gave her a call.

She is going to consolidate all of her ideas and papers into one place so she can start fresh and get the plan going. I recommended she start menu planning for 3 categories: takeaway family meals, soups, and sandwiches. I told her to narrow down her menu to 3 items on each category(9 total), and list the ingredients, their costs, how many servings she could make, and how many hours it would take her to make them. She will also reach out to the commissary kitchens locally to see what their rates are and if they are full.

AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM:

The business has reached its initial goal of 43 memberships, which is currently covering rent. However, sustainability is a concern due to anticipated seasonal slowdowns and potential member drop-offs. Although the business has just experienced its first break-even month, the margin was slim. Retaining members and bringing in new clients—especially during the slower summer months—will be critical.


RECOMMENDATIONS AND ACTIONS TO BE TAKEN:

  • Develop a referral program that rewards the person who refers with perks such as swag, gift cards, special access, or member-only hours.
  • Increase member retention by enhancing member benefits, such as new class offerings, special events, and exclusives.
  • Clearly outline and promote member benefits, including 1st class free, stretch classes, and perks tied to the Farmer’s Market.
  • Continue leveraging high-traffic days (Monday

Receipt of additional information from the business owners. Run 3 Internal Business Valuations through Valzy. The result is a range of values from $60,000 to $130,000 depending on various assumptions about future growth. Research on Indeed to estimate market salary for one of the two owners. Research on Loop.net to determine comparable rents.

Forward the results to Sam and Jeff:


Here are two Internal Business Valuations for The Canvas Company.  I ran the data through the automated software twice based on 2 different assumptions. 

The first one (The Canvas Company Inc.) estimates the value at $130,000. This value is based on the Projected Earnings Method. This method assumes projected revenue and projected Seller’s Discretionary Earnings (SDE) will grow over the next 3 year at 10% per year. This method present values the future SDE stream plus a terminal value to come up with the $130,000 value.

The second one (Revision 2) estimates a value of

Lance Wolfe <lwolfe@mtu.edu>

Analysis

Karen has recently been dealing with health issues, which has slowed her progress. An arm injury has also kept her from riding her horse—something that usually helps her recharge and stay motivated. Despite the setback, she’s been actively searching for an attorney to help protect her rights in journalism, showing that she’s still focused on her long-term goals.

Recommendation

I recommend that Karen continue nurturing her craft, especially during this downtime. Creating digital products like e-books, templates, or guides related to her skills could not only keep her creatively engaged but also help generate extra income.

Next Steps

  1. Focus on developing at least one digital product that showcases her expertise.
  2. Keep reaching out to potential attorneys to secure the support she needs for her journalism work.

AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM:

Client recently relocated her business to a new storefront in Marine City after closing her previous location in Waterford. Marine City is a seasonal tourist town, with peak traffic starting around April. The client was referred by the Chamber of Commerce.


  • The new location has underperformed in the off-season with only $4,000 in sales since January.
  • Monthly overhead is $2,000, with $1,300 going toward rent, creating financial pressure.
  • The client has $9,000 in personal loans from friends.
  • She was not charging sales tax prior to our meeting, creating potential compliance issues.
  • A cash register was purchased but remains unused.
  • There is little to no marketing in place and limited comfort with digital promotion, particularly on Facebook.


RECOMMENDATIONS AND ACTIONS TO BE TAKEN:

  • Sales tax setup completed in Square,
reviewed market research and forward to Dawn to discuss during our. next call.

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Analysis

Yanis recently attempted to secure a loan to support her business launch, but unfortunately, the banker determined the financials were too tight to approve it. This indicates a need to revisit her numbers, especially the assumptions around revenue, expenses, and growth projections. Despite the funding setback, she has moved forward with a soft opening of her location. However, traffic has been very slow, and the community doesn’t yet seem fully aware that she’s open for business.

Although she’s had some momentum, a soft opening alone isn’t enough to create buzz or drive customers through the door. A major grand opening—especially with support from LEDC, who is planning to do the ribbon-cutting—will be key to boosting visibility and giving her business the strong public launch it needs.

Recommendation

To generate awareness and drive traffic, I recommend running a series of limited-time specials or grand opening promo

Follow up on time tracking and strategy


Kayleigh did track her time for awhile and it caused her anxiety

She is working through a stressful job and is losing money on it

She had to stop the time tracking


The lesson learned was that communication with this client was poor

She has determined that email is the best method for client updates

The client continues to message and call with changes


Inquired if she has decided which type of business and projects she thinks she wants to pursue

Did not get a clear answer, but team apparel is occasional and not the mainstay

She likes mass customized work (create a pattern and sell it in different colors, etc)


Discussed different avenues of selling and their impact on time and money

If she rented a vendor space, there would be ongoing cost, but the ability to sell merchandise in a retail store

She does not want a regular cost outl

Along with John Schmitt, met with Brent via Zoom to review the work he had done in LivePlan.

  • Reviewed sections of the narrative he had completed. Lots of discussion amongst the 3 of us on the work he had done. All agreed it was complete except for the financial portion which is dependent on what type of purchase agreement he comes to with seller.
  • Reviewed the narrative. He had completed spreadsheet showing seasonality and expenses. He had reviewed the seller-supplied financials and gone through and eliminated or adjusted line items that would not be needed or changed.
  • After reviewing, discussed next steps. Summarized below.

Next Steps:

  • Jeff to complete financials for Brent to review. Mainly Revenue, Expenses, and Labor.
  • Brent to review, and decide what offer he wants to make.
  • Brent to contact seller agent and seller to discuss price, additional terms, and assistance with landlord and vendors.

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
CRITICAL ISSUE: They opened the restaurant, and I noticed on Facebook that they had some issues with their POS that was interrupting service in the bar. Sent an email to ask what it was and if I could help.

Hi David,


Looks like a flurry of activity up there from Facebook posts, I am so happy for your family.

We will be up to eat soon.


What kind of problems are you having with your POS? Is it software glitches or just getting used to working with it? I had two restaurants open this month, and both of you had issues with POS. Working on another business plan for someone purchasing an existing bowling alley/restaurant and they have no POS. They were still doing everything on paper. Would you recommend your POS, or would you choose another? 


No rush, just when you come up for air someday….


Sincerely, 


Lisa Kenny, Small Business Consultant


David called later in the day with

Laurisa,


Taking a look at your business plan, it occurs to me that this is written as though you are approaching investors. Business lending is much different, and I would like to structure the business plan more conventionally for the lending audience. Attached is a simple plan that we use. One thing I notice in your current plan is that everything is about the technology. I need you to flip this around and write it from the perspective of the business, rather than the techno

Follow up meeting with Chakiya to review her business plan. She is working with a SCORE mentor and using their business plan outline. She is working on another business plan that she wants reviewed too and I explained that she should focus on one business at a time to ensure success. She will consider my feedback for this plan (screenprinting) and potentially send me a draft for her other business idea (music artist). Will send new eRFC link if so. CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
I met with the client today for a follow-up session. Here is a summary of our meeting:


ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM

The client is making steady progress in growing her flower farm business while actively participating in the 2025 Cohort program. After relocating to a new property in April, she is now focused on establishing her new garden space and refining her business foundation. She is currently working with Business Soil, a marketing agency, to utilize her Cohort funds for the initial phase. While operations continue under the name Wooded White Cottage Farms, she recently registered Amanda Mays, LLC, based on recommendations from Business Soil. Her current focus includes branding, strengthening her marketing efforts, financial management, and fulfilling the requirements of the Cohort program. She has approximately $1,000 remaining in her Cohort funds, which she is considering applying toward CPA services for financial tools and support.

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes: Charmaine is about to open her Charcuterie trailer/cart. Berries and Boards is the business name. She has partnered with her sister and will open for business at the Farmer's Market on May 17th. This will be a soft opening with a grand opening to come. We discussed the planning, menu, transportation and the practicing for set up and order fulfillment is important prior to the opening. Each order is individually prepared including the mocktails. They are working on their timing and what can be prepared ahead of time (prep of fruit and vegetables etc.) Her marketing materials look great and she has completed the Can Do Kalamazoo Boot Camp for entrepreneurship and is excited to launch. I am not able to attend on the 17th but will reconnect with her after and do an after action review of how it went.

Webinar with Patrick; Reviewed other companies that Patrick had researched for the best selling wood/craft products, both online and locally. Client has some good ideas of good sellers, wooden bowls, boards, maybe pens. Check back in mid June.

Client noted that she is setting an alarm three times per day as a reminder to conduct a post on social media. We discussed the importance of having these cues.


Client noted that Facebook for business is now set up and operating.


She has also linked her Shopify account to her products and merchandise she is selling. We discussed the importance of building infrastructure at this stage of venture. The idea is that once the business begins to take off, she will have less time to focus on infrastructure, and that it makes most sense to focus on this now while business is still a little slow.


She now has her QuickBooks up and running as previously noted, but it is now linked to her checking account. She also now has a business Venmo account set up.

COMPETITOR INFORMATION

Rita Hillman <rhillma3@emich.

I met with Mindy on 5/8 to discuss business planning and walk her through LivePlan. Mindy updated me on her life - she recently lost her job at Soaring Eagle, which has caused her quite a bit of stress. She asked if the business is still possible. I advised that a lender would likely want to see that she has supplemental income to help with expenses and living, but it's not entirely impossible.

To get Mindy started, we walked through the narrative portion of LivePlan. She had a lot of questions about what should be included in each section, so I think the AI-generated custom instructions will come in handy. We talked through a few sections, and I also showed her how to update each section's status and add comments as needed.

She is going to take a few weeks to do the following:

  • Follow up with Montcalm Co. to get in touch with building department
  • Watch the start up courses through the LMS
  • Re-center and work through some of her business

Jaxen has been offered a content creation contract by Saginaw STARS but is a minor


The long and short of it is that STARS is a government entity and has criteria for contracts

Jaxen can not sign a contract until he is 18 in November

His father could form an entity and "hire" Jaxen to do the work

He is not sure his Dad will be open to that idea


Alternatively, Jaxen could find another entity that could accept the contract and hire him to complete the project


Suggested he ask STARS if they could re-hire him but pay him a bonus or special project fee in addition to regular wages


Jaxen may wait until November to form the company on his own, but is unsure how he will handle the summer project

He will talk to STARS so see what options they may have


Invited Jaxen to reach out with questions at any time

We had a quick check in today as Brendan has not been feeling well lately. His plan is to hit the streets next Monday with his brochure. He will be focusing on medical related businesses. He had his case meeting next Thursday for a more detailed update.

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Review email communication with client about rules ensuring understanding


﻿

First Consulting Session

Sandy has been in the salon business for 22 years, 10 as a business owner. She was referred to SBDC by John H. at Wildfire to establish a business plan. She indicated she has solid business financials that would support the $20K loan she is seeking for new equipment, coupled with having $5K down. She will be leasing a building (owned by husband) for $1K/mo. There is no escalation clause in the lease agreement because her husband is not insane. She will establish 6 chairs for other stylists that she will collect rent on. Will be starting out with 3 that are committed.


We went through a Word format business plan, the 3 year projection Excel file (she will get clarity from Lender how detailed this needs to be, if needed) and the excel bookkeeping format. Following up with attachments of all items along with highlight to link for additional training material on SBDC site. She acknowledged the Consent letter shortly after meeting.

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
PROBLEM


Fight stagnation and mental inertia






DISCUSSION


Revenue fuels growth - and innovation causes exponential scalability. I want to help you fortify your sales strategy for the 3rd and 4th quarter of 2025.


We are almost ALWAYS evolving through cycles of LIFE - let alone BUSINESS. I reached out today with a subtle nudge to affirm client remains ever thinking, dreaming - and DOING.






NEXT STEPS:


THINK BIGGER. Embrace that Scale - and build accordingly:

Two meetings with client on this day, each 2 hours, working on her financial forecast. Christie is working through several complex challenges related to financial modeling, revenue forecasting, and role categorization for her healthcare-related business. She’s navigating how to account for outsourced HR and accounting costs, clarify part-time and fractional employee compensation, and properly assign various roles to the correct cost categories. Additionally, Christie is refining a business model that includes a certified oncology nurse navigator, complex subscription-based revenue tied to patient enrollment, and a multi-phase development plan. She’s also preparing investor materials, including a submission to Henry Ford Innovations, and needs to align her forecast model with real-world sales timelines and customer acquisition cycles typical in healthcare. One particular challenge is modeling revenue based on the size and deal dynamics of health systems while planning for long vendor

Assisted with reviewing UIA correspondence and with paying amounts due on Q1 UIA. Quickbooks calculated 9000 as taxable wage base, UIA says it should be 9500. $14 difference. Otherwise, business is going well, and he will reach out when next needed.

First pass at business materials. A key differentiation is cited as billing code generation and competing systems are listed at $25K /year licenses in the competition section. My research turned up Commure which includes billing codes and prices in at $90/mo, so competitive differentiation needs to be better defined, adjusted or communicated. On another tack, the key hypothesis for this business is that small clinics will be accepting of an on prem "AI in a box" solution, so the requirements for any customer equipment administration need to be clear. Tech support by the company will be a scaling impediment, in terms of staffing, time, capital expense, etc. A review of customer discovery completed to date will inform these issues.

Analysis

Fatima has been busy juggling work and life responsibilities, which has left little time for focusing on her business. Recently, she expressed a desire to get back on track and reached out for support in doing so. During our meeting, we explored several manufacturing companies as potential partners for her product. We also discussed the idea of possibly rebranding or changing her business name to something more searchable and memorable—something that could make her easier to find online and better resonate with her target audience.

While she has strong ideas and clear goals, progress has been slow simply due to time constraints. However, with a few small but consistent actions, she can begin to build momentum again.

Recommendation

Given the time that has already spent researching manufacturers and the foundational ideas she’s developed, it’s important that she starts getting her business visible—especially on social

AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM:

Following a focused business retreat in April, it became clear that while significant progress has been made, there are inefficiencies in the sales and hiring processes that are impacting growth. The conversion rate from inquiry to close has dropped, though it still remains strong at 44% (50% when excluding non-ideal clients). The delay between inquiry and proposal delivery—averaging 10 days—correlates with slower hiring and client onboarding. The proposal process has now been delegated to a VA to increase efficiency.

Additionally, past issues with hiring (a podcast editor and a VA who were not the right fit) have slowed progress. There is also a need to refine the ad strategy for the upcoming academy cohort launch in September.


RECOMMENDATIONS AND ACTIONS TO BE TAKEN:

  • Continue refining the sales process by reducing the time between inquiry and proposal delivery (goal: unde

Yugo and Julia are working to get a clear financial picture for their AI-driven platform that helps solve technical problems in industrial sectors like nuclear, steel, and automotive. One of their biggest challenges is organizing historical financials, aligning the balance sheet with actuals from QuickBooks, and building a credible five-year financial model to support their $2M funding target. They’re also navigating complex issues like how to represent shareholder loans, categorize expenses, and forecast revenues from a mix of subscription and service-based pricing. While they’ve had early traction from pilot projects, Yugo is still seeking the right investor fit and is under pressure to build a full-time team that can execute on their growth plans.


I advised them to simplify where possible—especially when it comes to early revenue forecasting, compensation inputs, and categorizing costs. I walked them through how to use the spreadsheet as a planning tool, encourag

IMPACT REMINDER: new start coming.

Analysis

Kia is still working her current jobs while slowly trying to bring her daycare vision to life. Although she has started posting on social media, most of her content consists of photos from other sources rather than original pictures or content that reflect her unique brand or future daycare. At this point, her daycare center has not officially opened, and she hasn't yet secured a location or formal start date.

While she's showing initiative by staying active on social media, she needs to begin creating and sharing content that is personal, authentic, and aligned with her business goals. Parents want to feel connected and reassured, and building that trust starts now—even before the doors open.

Recommendation

To move her daycare forward, I recommended that she set a specific target date for opening and use it to guide her planning and outreach. Setting a clear goal creates urgency and structure. She also needs

Analysis

He has made solid progress on his business journey, most notably by improving his credit score to 650 with the help of becoming an authorized user. This improvement opens more doors for funding and financial opportunities. He has also found a potential new location for his business and is currently considering two sites—Green Dot and Cedar Street. While his ambition and drive are clear, managing two potential projects at once may be slowing down progress and spreading his focus too thin.

He has already submitted several key documents related to funding and business planning. These documents need further development, especially in terms of financial projections, to make his funding requests stronger and more credible. This is a critical step if he wants to move forward with either location successfully.<

Hello Jason. I hope that you are getting better. You had indicated that you were not well and that was one reason for not responding.

I am ready to help with either option and would appreciate hearing from you with an update.


Please let me know how you are doing and if I can help with anything at this time.


Best regards



Carl Bourdelais

Analysis

Tamaki has made significant strides by receiving and depositing the $20,000 from the Enhance Grant, which she’s now investing into building out her business. A portion of those funds is going directly toward developing her product line, which focuses on vitamins—specifically baby vitamins and hair vitamins. This marks an exciting new chapter in her business journey as she begins to solidify her brand identity and product offerings.

One key highlight is that she was able to find a baby avatar that perfectly represents her vitamin line. This visual branding element will help bring personality and recognition to her product, especially when targeting parents and caregivers looking for trustworthy, kid-friendly supplements.

Recommendation

To begin generating buzz and building her audience, I recommend she either start a dedicated Instagram page for the baby vitamin line or utilize her existing IG account—but with a new,

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes: Touching base with client to see progress - really has not moved company forward. Participating in some entrepreneurship eduacation and pitching but not really moving forward toward commercialization. The Mini case study seems to have fallen to the wayside.

I spoke with our client by telephone. She wanted guidance on reflecting a name change on her company. It was unclear what she wanted to do so I researched what it would take to change the name of the company at the state in LARA and then report it to the IRS. Instead she wanted to reflect her married name as the Resident Agent for the company. She also let me know that she would be moving in August of this year. I noted that she had not filed her Annual Statement for the company for 2025. We discussed that the filing would need to be done and later the address would need to be filed. She decided that she would wait and do all of it together in August after she moved. She also inquired about the BOIR and I was unsure whether she had to file an update given that there is no longer a requirement to register expect for foreign nationals operating a company in the US. I sent her a link to the BOI information page and suggested that she update her report so that there would not b

Britt and I discussed her business plan for her consulting business that helps individuals and organizations secure funding for indigenous farming. She has her own farm and has been considering turning her business into a nonprofit. She has been working with developers and MDARD, USDA and others. Our discussion ended with her deciding that since she already has a nonprofit status in addition to her LLC, she will continue her business as a nonprofit. Her SBDC case will be closed.

ClientIntake.CompanyName: Compassion by Heart Home Care Services LLC

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
Davina is running a small (1 client, her mom) home healthcare agency in addition to her FT day job as Care Coordinator. She'd like to expand the business and open a 'respite center' to provide a break for family care givers.


We discussed growing the home health side of the business first, and she intends to use her funding to work with an attorney and accountant to ensure that she is set up to contract with new clients and manage employees. Her plan is to live on her regular wages and use the money from the business to grow.


She also has a piece of property near the 'medical district' that she could develop into her respite center for ~$200k (based on a recent estimate). Alternatively, she might try to sell that to HFHS, which had offered to buy it from her grandmother several years ago.


She estimates that 1 employee can serve 3-5 clients and next time we'll do some financial planning to look at her

Client emailed with questions about financial ratios that the bank uses to evaluate loan applications. I sent him an explanation of current, quick and debt service coverage ratios along with calculations for each.

Daniel is developing a novel methane pyrolysis system to produce clean hydrogen and valuable carbon byproducts, but he’s navigating several complex technical and business challenges. One major issue is managing carbon buildup in the reactor without relying on traditional catalysts or molten metal columns, which can be inefficient or difficult to scale. Daniel’s unique approach uses helical augers to mechanically remove carbon, a design he believes is not found elsewhere in the industry. He’s also working to finalize a working prototype, estimate the cost of scaling the system by 10x, and refine the unit economics—particularly understanding how to price hydrogen production and evaluate the role of carbon as a revenue stream. Another challenge lies in simplifying the technical process for investors, while still showcasing the system’s unique advantages.


I recommended that Daniel focus on producing a clean and compelling process flow diagram to help potential investors

Christie reached out asking for assistance with creating an opening day balance sheet for her SBA loan. She will end up with two loans, one for $150K and the other for the remaining $630K. Christie is going to send us the contact information of her loan officer at ChoiceOne Bank so that Harry and I can speak with her. PROBLEM


Fight stagnation and mental inertia






DISCUSSION


Revenue fuels growth - and innovation causes exponential scalability. I want to help you fortify your sales strategy for the 3rd and 4th quarter of 2025.


We are almost ALWAYS evolving through cycles of LIFE - let alone BUSINESS. I reached out today with a subtle nudge to affirm client remains ever thinking, dreaming - and DOING.






NEXT STEPS:


THINK BIGGER. Embrace that Scale - and build accordingly:


PLEASE - checkout this short video, affirming the reality that YOU only have so much time - so build your strategy with the FOCUS that allows you to SCALE (and not BURN OUT)


https://youtu.be/V8h64ePj-0k?si=9B-Id-eXaUgjb_W-






LEVERAGE THIS ONLINE CLASS TO BOL

PROBLEM

Help with business planning


DISCUSSION

1. Business Plan

a. Curious observations from business plan

i. Market research to affirm more people prefer a home feel vs large facility; shocked because most people don’t really know this option

ii. Employee retention – how do we get and keep good employees? (one of the weakest aspects of this industry)

1. Happy, love what they are doing, constant training, incentives

iii. Authenticity - They Want to reframe the working and language of this business plan to their personal values, tone and authenticity.

iv. Insurance – their insurance guy through some curveballs for insurance – ie…even 1099 contractors, must add them as additionally insured on their liability insurance.


2. House

a. Price was $285k and seller wouldn’t budge despite their desire to reduce by $3

Summary

Greg and Michele, 


As I mentioned in my previous email, I was going to connect you to a representative from the Small Business Development Center (SBDC) to learn what steps need to be taken to get your business certified. 


I have CC'd Julie Oldham, who works with businesses on their finances, succession planning and so forth.

Best, 

Thank you Julie,


I will proceed on the paperwork now that we have concluded our ribbon cutting.


Here are the links to learn more of

Nikki is working through several growth and financial challenges as she develops her real estate technology platform. She’s facing key decisions about when to start charging users, how to price services for both buyers and sellers, and how to scale across zip codes and eventually states. She’s also navigating the complexity of projecting user growth, defining what constitutes a “sale,” estimating marketing and operational expenses, and determining the capital needed to hit her revenue goals. Additionally, Nikki is preparing for a $250,000 fundraising round and needs to clearly communicate her growth strategy, pricing model, and financial trajectory to potential investors.


I encouraged Nikki to refine her pricing model to emphasize perceived value while testing buyer willingness through early trials and opt-in features. We discussed using a steady monthly growth formula to model expansion realistically and advised focusing early monetization efforts on high-interest

Client responded to competitive positioning wrt Commure:


Thanks Joe,


I am familiar with commure. My mother did a demo of the product and I have access to a free subscription for a demo period.


There are a few problems with commure as well as other current scribe technologies.


For a few reasons the Commure solution will not work

  • You can not do simultaneous sessions
  • You can not import your own complex templates easily

Commure is great for doctors that see patients in a order in a single thread. For specialists that talk to the patient for the duration of the visit then move on to the next, this is a great option. For doctors that see patients in a parallel fashion where they have multiple MAs in different rooms working simultaneously, this solution will not work. You can only have one scribe session going, not multiple at the same time. You would have to pause the session to move to a di

Will likely readdress business planning options as they get closer to a target opening date. Went over some part of the document which were completed to date.

AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM:

Business continues to decline, she's working more hours as she can't afford payroll. She has the business listed with a relator, but they aren't a business broker so she's seen very little action. She's not really telling customers yet as she doesn't want them to wait for discounted items. She's moving old inventory on posh mark. She's not making much on it if breaking even.

She look into BizBuySell as it might be an option.

she will look into it or just run her inventory out

My first meeting with client, detailed dig into tech and overview of customer discovery.


Introduction and Name Clarification

  • Olakunle confirmed the pronunciation and mentioned that they can be called Mike.
  • There was a brief discussion about the confusion due to both participants having the same name Mike.


Company Overview and Product Description

  • Olakunle and Mike introduced their fintech company called Operabis.
  • Operabis aims to help people manage their subscriptions proactively through a rule-based system.
  • Customers can set rules to cancel subscriptions, block free trials, and receive alerts.
  • The company is currently building a prototype and plans to launch it with minimum features next week.

Analysis

Lissy is currently balancing an intense workload, managing three jobs just to maintain her income. With such limited time and energy, her ability to focus on growing her business has understandably taken a back seat. Although she’s still passionate about her IT business, progress has been slow due to her demanding schedule. In addition to her business goals, she is also working on a community-focused initiative, which reflects her strong commitment to making a broader impact.

Despite these challenges, she remains motivated and continues to look for ways to keep her business moving forward, even if it's at a slower pace for now.

Recommendation

Summary: The client sent me the following 3 institutes from NIH that he is interested in: “· National Institute of Child Health and Human Development (NICHD): Relevance: NICHD supports rehabilitation research for individuals with physical disabilities, including mobility aids to improve functional independence. Their device’s focus on self-operated transfers and standing aligns with NICHD’s Eunice Kennedy Shriver Intellectual and Developmental Disabilities Research Centers, which fund assistive technologies. Why Choose NICHD: Their prior IRB research (DMC, March 2024) and VA LOIs (April 23, 2025) demonstrate feasibility, and NICHD’s broad rehabilitation scope fits their Phase I prototyping goals. NICHD is actively soliciting SBIR applications for assistive devices (PA-24-245). Contact: Louis Quatrano (louis.quatrano@nih.gov, 301-402-2242) for pre-application guidance. Ben Richey

Yugo is working to refine his financial projection with the ambitious goal of reaching $20 million in revenue by 2029, but he's facing challenges in expense categorization, realistic revenue forecasting, and appropriate investment levels in R&D and marketing. One area of concern was the platform subscription numbers, which showed signs of cyclical behavior, as well as the risk of double-counting resubscriptions. Additionally, some of the operating costs like legal, HR, and marketing were initially understated, leading to unrealistically high profit margins. Yugo was also unsure how to benchmark his spending across these categories relative to industry norms.


I suggested adjusting the spreadsheet formulas to avoid double-counting and offered to help refine the cost allocations. We talked through the SaaS operating benchmarks and I shared a helpful article to guide future decisions. I also encouraged more aggressive investment in marketing and software develop

ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM

The client is entering a transitional phase in her business, with both she and her bookkeeper planning to retire in approximately two years. While she has made strides in improving financial reporting and employee retention, the business still faces operational challenges, especially with supplier management and the complexity of managing four locations. She is seeking guidance on updating her business plan and preparing the company for sale, as no internal succession plan currently exists. A key issue is how to streamline systems and operations while ensuring the business is well-positioned and valued correctly for a successful exit.

Case prep: review of client intake and case files.


Case Report:


Sarah Mathew and Sarah Koetje are both business and life partners. They are interested in setting up an LLC and domain name. Their business will offer daycamp primarily to individual individuals ages six through 12 as well as seniors. They’re interested in providing unique outings which are both experiential and place based learning opportunities.


The clients both previously worked for the little Traverse Bay Conservancy, where they provided similar. We talked at length about potential partnerships in the region, including intermediate school districts, and private education institutions.


We also discussed insurance needs for the business as well as potential liabilities they could incur. They had been working with an insurance agent out of Traverse City however they were having a difficult time, assessing their risk and liability. We discussed several

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM:

I reviewed Lorinda's P&Ls and Balance Sheet. The value is in her inventory and assets at this point. It's clean and there's a small profit each year, but finding a buyer might be a challenge.


Her lease is up on her building and the landlord hasn't requested a renewal yet. She is nervous to tell him as he will want to move quickly to fill the space. She thinks she might run out of inventory in July.


RECOMMENDATIONS AND ACTIONS TO BE TAKEN:

I suggested she look into BizBuySell and see if there's a privacy standard she can set, but she needs to do that right away as her inventory is low. We talked about running it out to the EDA but her landlord is on the board.


I suggested that she check her lease to see how long of a notice she needs to give and consider giving it when she turns in June's rent.


NEXT STEPS & FOLLOW-UP ACTIONS:

S

In-business update


Sam Augusta <saugusta@michigansbdc.org>

Hello Berea,


It has been a while since I reached out to you about scheduling an appointment. Are you still interested in pursuing a business in Michigan? Do you still want business consulting from the MiI SBDC? If you do please give me a few days and times that work for you the week of May 19th. We can get a phone or Zoom meeting scheduled.

Automate Detroit 2025 on site, we conducted customer discovery on the floor. Attendees ranged from small businesses to Fortune 500 level. Two possible client profiles with a sense of urgency are emerging, one for failing production line technology quick fixes replaced with more substantial proven tech, and R&D groups tasked with specific assignments. Most attendees are in browsing mode, not trying to solve specific problems. Most of the current methods for identifying new tech is old school google search, trade show discovery and colleague referrals. Not many are using LLMs frequently on the job. Scott posed some customer validation questions after the discovery ones and a broad description of his idea for a tool was met with positive responses. The sales cycle is shaping up to be extended, involving committee approval, etc. for sizable companies, as anticipated.

Summary:


Me  and Chad engaged in a detailed discussion regarding a potential business acquisition, focusing on the asking prices of $250,000 for the business and $400,000 for the associated real estate. Chad, having signed a non-disclosure agreement, plans to analyze the financial documents he received, which include profit and loss statements and balance sheets. They discussed negotiating terms such as seller financing and the inclusion of equipment in the sale, with Me emphasizing the importance of understanding the financials to evaluate the investment's viability. Chad expressed interest in acquiring all equipment, contingent on the overall pricing and value assessment.


The conversation also covered financing options for the acquisition, with Me suggesting commercial financing and SBA loans, while Chad shared his available capital of $25,000. They identified the need for a comprehensive purchase package that includes real estate and equipmen

Held a follow-up session with the client today.


ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM

The client is making steady progress in the early growing season while participating in the 2025 Cohort program. Despite weather delays and a recent illness, she has been active at the Caro Farmers Market. She is moving forward with applying for the MDARD grant I sent her a few weeks ago. She needs to revisit and update her Cohort milestones, formalize her business structure, and strategically invest her remaining Cohort funds, with a focus on marketing and basic financial tools. As her operation expands, securing grant funding, establishing partnerships, and enhancing financial systems will help ensure long-term sustainability.


SUMMARY OF BUSINESS IDEA AND TOPICS DISCUSSED

She has planted 50% of her gardens for the 2025 season and brought early crops, including salad greens, spinach, rhubarb, and baby potatoes, to sell at the opening day of the Caro Farmers

Summary: The client wrote that her green card process is moving faster than expected, and there’s a good chance she’ll be starting the final steps within the next two months. She wanted to know if I have heard back from NSF yet as she’d love to submit her pitch and get the project started as soon as possible.


Action: I responded that is great news. Fingers crossed this next part goes smoothly. My meeting with NSF last week was completely non-informational. They just kept repeating to all involved, that everything was currently going as normal beyond the changes already made (they can view all of the information at https://www.nsf.gov/executive-orders and https://www.nsf.gov/updates-on-priorities). And they just continually referred to those two websites. However, within 24 hours of that call, I got wind that they were going to stop external reviews again and they were going to freeze funding. Additionally, this article was published in Science (https://www.science.

Received an email from Ashley asking to meet. Review notes from sessions in early 2024 to refresh my memory regarding this client. Return email suggesting we meet by zoom May 13 mid-day.

AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM:

The business is experiencing a significant slowdown, with sales from January to March being the lowest in the past five years. This aligns with a broader industry downturn. Although last year’s revenue was $6.3M, the owner anticipates a flat year with a target of $4–4.5M. Despite this, there’s a desire to maintain current staffing levels (24 employees, 2 part-time) and eventually grow to $8M annually. Profitability is being impacted by recent investments in the building and raised PTO expenses (up $80K).


RECOMMENDATIONS AND ACTIONS TO BE TAKEN:

  • Send and review the current P&L to identify core expense drivers.
  • Formalize the strategic marketing plan on paper; current efforts include updated website, billboards, and direct mail, but lack structure.
  • Evaluate SEO effectiveness and explore potential with Google Ads (not currently in use).
  • Revisit SOPs and b

Mohamed is working on launching a gunshot detection system for schools but is navigating multiple challenges. He’s collaborating with a co-founder who is highly risk-averse and cautious about adopting new strategies, which slows decision-making and hesitancy around vendor partnerships. Mohamed is also balancing technical development and sales strategy, trying to determine how much to build before piloting, while ensuring the product stands out from competitors. The system detects gunshots within one second and provides real-time location data, which is a strong differentiator, but integrating it into existing school alert systems and overcoming the conservative nature of school purchasing remain hurdles. He’s also preparing a pitch for upcoming opportunities and wants to improve his delivery style and presentation structure.


I recommended that Mohamed focus on pushing forward with pilots using the current hardware rather than waiting for cosmetic improvements, and t
Reviewed business plan proposal, quotes, final items for grant submission. Meeting again next week

Christie is navigating several complexities in launching her new healthcare venture, particularly around structuring development phases, calculating operational costs, and modeling revenue from different-sized healthcare systems. She’s working with multiple vendors—Redox, Alpha Django, and Nefrodata—and needs clarity on fee structures, timelines, and documentation. Pricing the nurse navigator service and managing headcount across systems are also areas of uncertainty. Christie is trying to balance affordability, staffing quality, and scalability, while also revising her training and subscription fee models to better reflect patient volume. Additionally, she's working to align the financial projections with the phased development timeline and ensure her model accurately reflects upfront and ongoing revenue streams.


I helped Christie think through how to categorize vendor costs, calculate the proper pricing tiers for different-sized entities, and structure her fin

    1. Agnew signage is going to work on a quote for removing the sign and adding framing.

She also hosted an art event, where other artists could display and sell their art. It was well attended and a great community event. Nicolena was proud of her efforts with this. She has about $2,000 into this event and plans to do this as an annual event. She will be looking for sponsorship in the future. The event was called Blooming Black Eden. She shared photos of the event.


We discussed her plans for the building she recently purchased and it sounds like her and her partner plan to create a welcome center, where you can purchase pre-packaged treats but also purchase items from multiple makers in the area. They are also considering a bait shop at this location. She is currently looking at funding opportunities at this location. Her uncle Paul Collins who is a famous per

David reached out and said that he is pursuing an opportunity  for funding that has requested he turn in invoices as proof that he is in business.  He asked if we could help him develop an invoice template as well as create two invoices for work that h

Initially referred by Janis Seabolt at AA Spark, I spoke with her on March 4 for background, and had a Zoom scheduled with Rolf later that afternoon. He joined with just 5 minutes to go, so it was rescheduled to March 6. I sent him TT on-boarding link. He contacted me again in early May. We had a follow up Zoom on May 12. He hadn't submitted the eRFC, but with a nudge he completed it later that day. Rolf is has an academic background. He is working "iron caramelization" which is an old technology that extracts iron from recycled metal as a vapor then turned to powder. He is intrigued by possible uses in low earth orbit manufacturing and ultimately metallurgy on Mars (hence the company name - Two Planet Steel). He received a Phase I SBIR grant from NSF, but funds are running out. He is working on a Phase II application due in early July. He said he completed a regional I-Corps program for the Phase I but didn't get much out of it. He hasn't done much rigorous

Ben sent an email to let me know that they had met with Bill and Jessica at Huntington and that the loan request seems to be approved but at a lower level that anticipated.


I replied letting him know that the approval would be positive and that they will need to prove the process to provide justification for a larger amount.


I did some research and provided information and links to the SBA Export Working Capital program and will be ready to assist as needed if a new loan package needs to be developed.


email exchange:

Ben, this is not bad news. At least you can fill the first contract of the trial works.\ and build credibility slowly.

 

I was hoping that they might have been able to do more.

Need to talk about capacity with him, to know what his max revenues and time expenditure could be.

Shared business plan templates with client at his request, notified team of his intention to continue with SBDC after our three sessions

Finished first module, added two more.


Trying to get into Trinity Health and Ascension Health.


Client may have colleagues with interest in investing in a couple of client companies. I expect to hear more in the near future.


Given the focus on med-tech, I will introduce to Shruti.

Next time we meet in 2 weeks we will review her Google business listing.

Client was referred to Wendy by another client and by Beth Houtrow of Kzen. Client is now planning to buy a small building to host group sessions and individual sessions as well as renting it out to other users. Space is 1,200 s.f. so group size is limited. (coworking/collaborative event space business model)


Client's husband lost job in Nov. '24 so that's a challenge with the lender. However, they have $150K cash on hand and subject building is asking $258,000 (realtor thinks they could negotiate to $200,000). May need to seek loan of $70,000 so that they have some operating cash on hand for the first months of operation. (perhaps more, need to do projections to verify)

Zoom call with Nikki and Jay. They won the 5X5 pitch competition, and said the changes Scott and I recommended were increibly helpful. They are continuing to work on product development, and noted that ther developers are running behind promised deadlines. They are seeing more signups by professionals but still struggling to reach individual homebuyers. Discussed additional ideas on how to create marketing outreach.


They also discussed the status of their investor relations. There original goal was to finish the round by April 29, but so far have only raised $25K. They are continuing to meeting with MI Rise, Red Cedar and IDVentures, but discussions are not moving with any urgency or high level of interest. They are finding a lot of investors have been burnt by PropTech, which is creating reticence in investing in their company. Discussed strategies as well as their need to bring in short tem income.

Nicole is working on applying for the MDARD Underserved Rural Populations Grant that is due June 11, 2025 at 5pm. She found a grant writer who has received funding to help applicants apply for MDARD funding. She is connecting with her later this week to go over the questions she has.


This is her application she is working on. I suggested she create a google document, copy all the questions into the document, type her responses into the doc so she can save them for future grants she may apply for.

Heidi Best, the client is working with a building owner and Coney Island owners (downtown Kalamazoo) on starting a bar. I co consulted with John Schmitt and his recommendation was an attorney in Grand Rapids, Michael Brown. He is an expert in working with the MLCC. Heidi and I have also been working on her financing. She has explored business loans and they have been difficult for her. She is planning to use her home for a HELOC. I met with Jessica at Mid's Harrison Campus to discuss the policies that she laid out further. She has several clients whose living situations have changed since they signed their contracts. These changes have added time needed to clean the houses, and Jessica is having a difficult time addressing the issue. We discussed one client in particular who is having health issues and can no longer keep the house tidy enough for a deep clean. I suggested approaching the subject as a concern for the client's well-being, stressing that Jessica wants to make sure that their home is healthy and clean.

We also worked on new policies that would require a re-evaluation if conditions in the house change. We ran some ideas through ChatGPT to see what it would come up with.

I can tell that Jessica is quite stressed about keeping her clients. However, I reminded her that boundaries are important to establish now before things get out of hand and completely unmanageable.

I invited our client to the MMDC luncheon tomorrow. She had just registered yesterday. Missed it by that much! Email: https://michigansbdcatgrandvalleystateuniv.lightning.force.com/lightning/r/EmailMessage/02sVs00000RKSNCIA5/view

My initial and quick follow-up to our meeting on 5/12:


From: infinitecs@earthlink.net <infinitecs@earthlink.net>

Sent: Monday, May 12, 2025 12:22 PM

To: 'rebekahspureliving@gmail.com' <rebekahspureliving@gmail.com>

Subject: RE: Follow Up to this Morning's Meeting


Hi Rebekah!

 

It was great to catch up with you this morning! I’m happy to hear things are well.

ClientIntake.BusinessType: Real Estate and Rental and Leasing

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
I sent Babatunde Ajao and Rodney Dickerson a message regarding their business plan:

I referred your business plan to our high tech person Arcadio Ramirez, and he hasn't returned my call or text. I will see him at a conference in a few days and will inquire about your package. In the meantime, I would prefer to refer you to a couple of lenders who might be able to help. Please keep in mind that since your business is a startup, the most that you might be able to get in a loan is $50,000, unless you have collateral and depending on your credit score; however, you should contact them for a discussion:

  1. Invest Detroit - Derek Edwards, 313.566.8224
  2. Lawrence Technical University - Dennis Shaver, 720.530.7970, dshaver@ltu.edu
  3. Opportunity Resources Center Fund - Carlton Shelton, 313.462.

Analysis of the problem:


Client was referred by her credit union so she can become aware of SBDCs as she works on growing her business. She's not looking to take out a loan; however, at some point she would like to build business credit.


Misty is currently getting business word of mouth, through social media, some affiliate relationships, and Parks & Recreation, for Oakland Township and Fenton. Although she's making some headway these are small rec centers.


We talked about her ability to contact additional recreation centers.


We discussed her perhaps hosting some indoor courses because there are times when weather can impact her planned client activities. For example, heavy rains, or cold weather. Schoolcraft college is just one option to market to their Recreation Department to offer the course because they have a very well attended Aquatics program. Other Community Colleges could be similar.

<

ClientIntake.CompanyName: Maia Nelson Photography LLC- TBD Start

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes: Worked on functions in wave bookkeeping options of Venmo payments vs wave cost/benefit. More discussion on contracts/deposits/cancellation policies. Client will open bank account for biz today and we will test functionality. In the future will continue work on billing and record keeping. Potential Google listing improvements and pricing.

AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM:

Scoreboard is actively growing and was recently accepted into a cohort program. The business has made key operational investments and is working on internal workflow improvements.


Current embroidery processes are leading to delays and inefficiencies, prompting a need for an overhaul. Team structure and task management inconsistencies have also impacted productivity. There is a need for clearer delegation, better scheduling, and improved internal communication, especially as larger orders come in.


RECOMMENDATIONS AND ACTIONS TO BE TAKEN:

  • Follow up with Stacy regarding cohort participation and resources available.
  • Implement embroidery process changes to reduce lead times and increase output.
  • Maintain daily check-ins with Jason to streamline communication and leadership.
  • Reassign or refine Amber’s

Introductory meeting discussing pricing models, customer discovery and beachhead market targeting. Alexander discussed the progress of his company's SBIR applications, marketing strategies, and the development of a diagnostic system for machinery systems. We discussed the potential applications of their product in various industries, including maritime diagnostics, manufacturing, and defense, and the challenges of pricing and marketing their product. The importance of understanding the value chain, targeting urgent problems in business, and approaching potential customers with an open-ended approach were also emphasized. They are tied into Spark and will follow up when the SBIR funding is clarified. Their plan B if the SBIRs fall through is to self fund through consulting for Maritime applications. The tech transfer agreement with UofM is in the final stages and is nailing down loose ends, but has general approval and was

Chris doing some outreach to gauge interest in his weightlifting assistance invention.

Three gyms seem reasonably interested.

Discussion today starting about logistics - how to sell it, set it up, train users, etc.


Chris will lay out his thoughts and we will vet together next week.

ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM:

Staffing remains a challenge as the farmer’s market season begins. Current coverage relies heavily on Donna, with additional support from Sandee’s sister and occasional shifts from Sandee and Chris. Scheduling needs to be formalized to ensure consistent coverage and avoid burnout.


RECOMMENDATIONS AND ACTIONS TO BE TAKEN:

  • Continue recruiting efforts to build a more reliable weekend team.
  • Develop a formal shift schedule to organize current staff availability and plan ahead.
  • Monitor staffing levels weekly to adjust as market demand fluctuates.


NEXT STEPS & FOLLOW-UP ACTIONS:

  • Draft and implement a rotating shift schedule that includes all active staff.
  • Evaluate staffing needs after the next two weekends to determine hiring urgency.
  • Track progress of custom work at the Boat House to ensure timely deliv

AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM:

The client remains overwhelmed with a backlog of work and has not made progress on hiring since the last meeting. A potential administrative candidate has submitted a resume, but the client is stuck in decision-making. His tendency to overthink long-term needs—such as requiring QuickBooks skills—creates hesitation in moving forward with immediate hiring needs. Additionally, delays in receiving quotes from Allegra are putting potential grant funding opportunities at risk.


RECOMMENDATIONS AND ACTIONS TO BE TAKEN:

  • Post the job as an entry-level role with “room for growth,” targeting self-motivated candidates to ease pressure around long-term expectations.
  • Review and follow up on the admin resume to move the hiring process forward.
  • Contact Scoreboard N More, a newly approved vendor, as a backup to Allegra for timely quotes.


NEXT STEPS &a

ClientIntake.CompanyName: Adam & Karmel LLC dba A&K Laundry Basket

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
I met with our client via zoom call to review her draft of her submission for the HUB grant. We reviewed the form and the different parts that she needs to work on to ready it for submission. She has made a great deal of headway given the limited time she has available to work on it.


We discussed laying out each request under the $20K umbrella:

  • about $4557 per washing machine (21 lb. capacity) times two machines for $9114. She currently has 4 - 40 lb. capacity washers available and believes that more would use the smaller capacity if available.
  • $7,000 for a marketing campaign that includes radio and digital advertising. She has been told to expect in excess of a 10 to one ratio - revenue dollars to dollars spent on advertising. This may be true, but it does not coincide with my experience with radio advertising. I think her greatest impact could be in offering commercial laundry services (drop off and pick up) for many area businesses.

Hello George!

Mariah Goos <mgoos

I met with our client by telephone. She currently works as a therapist for a private company working with emotional support for children. She has 10 years experience working with children and their emotional health. We talked about her plan to create a Community Living Support company offering in home care and services.


There is apparently a long waiting list for companies to provide support for children. The children are qualified by an assessment from a caseworker at Community Mental Health (CMH). Typical referrals are for support at a level of 30 to 40 hours each month. Those with a SED (serious emotional disturbances) waiver can receive up to 300 hours of support. Link to Health & Human Services website: https://www.michigan.gov/mdhhs/keep-mi-healthy/mentalhealth/mentalhealth/medwaivers/ch

Continued work with client on exploratory meetings with potential clients.

Because she is a librarian she is a very good researcher and a very good writer. Now we need to push boundaries and she needs to have some meetings where she tries to sell her services. She is 90% ready so needs to go do a meeting or two before we make any adjustments.

Subject: RE: Yeti Services LLC - Final Business Packet

ClientIntake.BusinessType: Real Estate and Rental and Leasing

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes: The request is now complete.

Summary

Subject: Need customs help

 

Hi Kris. I’m having to suspend my sales to other countries…mostly Canada and Australia because my customers are now getting hit with exorbitant taxes and customs brokerage fees. I’ve been shipping this way for 15 years, but my customers used to be exempt from import taxes for under $600. Not with the new tariff wars. I’m attaching one list of charges for a $125 order. I called UP. The customs fee was because now packages are assigned to brokers at the border who are charging crazy high fees. From what I’m reading, I may be able to save money by finding a customs broker in each country I ship to, but I’m not sure I sell enough to make that feasible. I was not expecting this huge disruption this week, as my time is very limited to figure this out.

PROBLEM

Staffing revolt brewing


DISCUSSION

Executive Chef is backing her into a corner, demanding pay increase from $65k to $90k - fueled by mindfulness of her growth in sales

Cooking staff unrest - as wait staffer received $500+ in tips one day and they feel underpaid


NEXT STEPS

  1. standardize your processes, recipes,
  2. create metrics and KPIs that can empower team to take ownership of revenue driving activity - possible consider performance based incentives for staff (quarterly? monthly?)
  3. hire additional chef
  4. use catering as a way to interview new chefs on short term/project specific basis as a means to vet, build database of future team members

Summary

Hi Julie just keeping in touch. I think I am holding off on the sawmill right now. The owner doesn't seem like he will budge in pricing and I think my better option right now is to get a Woodmizer sawmill and go later later on in life. Wolverine said they will work with me either way I go. Trying to get a LOC to help get me going if not I'll have to do it the hard way and only buy the woodmizer with a loan and work my way up

Hi Jacob,


Thanks for the update. I think you are smart to hold off especially since the owner will not budge. The price is too expensive.


Does Woodmizer sawmill have a plan for payment plans to purchase the sawmill? Sometimes they will work with you. Please let me know if you need assistance with this.


Enjoy your day!

I had not heard back from Joseph following my email with details about my search for a lender, so I sent a request for an update.


Joseph called and explained that he had read my email to say that a loan was not possible. I explained that I had not meant to convey that only that it may be more challenging due to him having a golf course. I convinced him to give Citizens National and Huntington (Michelle) a call to see about their interest in his expansion project. I forwarded Michelle's contact info to him right away.


I asked him to let me know how the conversations go and to let me know if I can help with anything.

We met to review the final version of her financial plans and business plans as finalized by Peter, her SCORE consultant. The business plan is 30 pages and very complete with market data and chef biography as well as an analysis of the competition. The logo and branding is complete and also in the plan. After careful review and reworking the math on the financials, she submitted the plan to Tami Pitale, lender at Honor CU for review. She will reach out when she hears from Honor CU.

ClientIntake.CompanyName: Total Venture LLC dba Brothers Hydrovac

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
AN ANALYSIS OF THE CRITICAL PROBLEM: She needs general help expanding

 

RECOMMENDATIONS AND ACTIONS TO BE TAKEN: We Zoomed to update her case. She has been busy lately with issues around her attempt to purchase a property in SC. This fell through. She has 4 truckers now and needs more space. She has a new quote to SOM for about $1.75MM. She is slightly ahead of last year's revenues. She hired 2 new guys. She could use a new bank connection...maybe Choice One, where she banks now. Her credit is above 700 now. She has gotten a few trucks with fines... yuck. She is looking at other facilities.

 

NEXT STEPS & FOLLOW-UP ACTIONS: I will send Choice

CounselorRecord.CounselorNotes:
A - Reviewed the plan that we discussed last meeting. Doug still seems to have no sense of urgency. Very little follow up on any of the items we discussed as a strategy for getting short term business. As of June 1 they will have lost $17k in monthly recurring revenue with no backfill for that revenue. They have not followed up on securing a line of credit. They have enough cash to go through mid-July.

R - recommendations in the session document we work on in their system. Follow up with clients who have recently left to see of they are happy with their service. focus on windows 11 upgrade, network and present

N - follow up meeting at the end of May
